perturbation.original_text: 8. Non Solicit. Each Party agrees that for a period of two (2) years from the date hereof, neither Party will, without the prior written consent of other, directly or indirectly solicit for employment or hire any employee, consultant, officer or director of the other Party with whom the Party has had contact or who became known to the Party in connection with the Proposed Transaction. Notwithstanding the foregoing, a Party shall not be precluded from hiring any such employee, consultant, officer or director who (i) responds to any public advertisement placed by the Party or (ii) has been terminated by the other Party prior to commencement of employment discussions between the Party and such employee, consultant, officer or director.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Non Solicit. Each Party agrees that for a period of five (5) years from the date hereof, neither Party will, without the prior written consent of other, directly or indirectly solicit for employment or hire any employee, consultant, officer or director of the other Party with whom the Party has had contact or who became known to the Party in connection with the Proposed Transaction. Notwithstanding the foregoing, a Party shall not be precluded from hiring any such employee, consultant, officer or director who (i) responds to any public advertisement placed by the Party or (ii) has been terminated by the other Party prior to commencement of employment discussions between the Party and such employee, consultant, officer or director.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years may be deemed unreasonably long and thus an unenforceable restraint on trade under California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Non-solicitation agreements can be seen as a restraint on trade. While some limited exceptions exist, a blanket five-year non-solicitation agreement is likely to be considered an unreasonable restraint on trade and unenforceable in California, especially considering the original contract is in Virginia, not California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from two to five years, which the Perturbation Explanation suggests is an unreasonable restraint on trade under California law. The Law Explanation reinforces this by stating that a blanket five-year non-solicitation agreement is likely to be considered an unreasonable restraint on trade and unenforceable in California. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), explicitly states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The extended non-solicitation period in the changed text directly contradicts this law by imposing a potentially unenforceable restraint on trade, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 12. Term. The term of this Agreement shall be for a period of two (2) years from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. Term. The term of this Agreement shall be for a period of ten (10) years from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the agreement's term to ten years could be viewed as an unreasonably long duration for a confidentiality agreement, depending on the nature of the information and the industry.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (VUTSA) doesn't prescribe a specific time limit for confidentiality agreements, it focuses on the protection of trade secrets. A ten-year term for a confidentiality agreement, especially if it covers information that may become outdated or publicly available sooner, might be challenged as an unreasonable restraint of trade, particularly if the information is not a trade secret or if the agreement unduly restricts competition beyond what is necessary to protect legitimate business interests. The courts would consider whether the duration is reasonably related to the lifespan of the protected information and the potential harm from its disclosure.

perturbation.justification: The change extends the agreement term significantly, which, according to the provided Law Explanation, could be challenged as an unreasonable restraint of trade under VUTSA. While Snippet 1 focuses on a repealed law (Virginia Paint Law), the Law Explanation emphasizes that a ten-year term might be deemed unreasonable, especially if the information covered becomes outdated or if it unduly restricts competition beyond what's necessary to protect legitimate business interests. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because the extended term could violate state law regarding reasonable restraints of trade, making the changed contractual term potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia and any legal action hereunder may be brought in an appropriate federal or state court located in Fairfax County, Virginia. The prevailing Party in any such action shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs incurred in any such action.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by laws of the State of California and any legal action hereunder may be brought in an appropriate federal or state court located in Fairfax County, Virginia. The prevailing Party in any such action shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs incurred in any such action.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying California law as the governing law while maintaining Virginia as the forum creates a conflict, potentially violating choice of law principles if the contract has stronger ties to Virginia.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification introduces a conflict because while parties are generally free to choose the governing law in their contracts, that choice is not absolute. The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 states that the chosen law will be applied unless (a) it is contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state, and (b) application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of the state. Here, by designating California law while retaining Fairfax County, Virginia as the forum, there's a potential conflict if Virginia has a materially greater interest in the contract (due to the principal place of business of one of the parties being in Virginia, as well as the initial contract being in Virginia). If Virginia law has policies that differ significantly from California law regarding the subject matter of the contract, the choice of California law may be unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies California law as the governing law while maintaining Virginia as the forum. The Law Explanation states that choice of law is not absolute and cites Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, noting that the chosen law will not be applied if it conflicts with a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this creates a conflict if Virginia has stronger ties to the contract. The absence of relevant law snippets does not negate this contradiction, as the Law Explanation provides a valid legal basis for the conflict.
